title: SEGMENT 4 — Star alleles and metabolizer phenotypes

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Star alleles and metabolizer phenotypes
So how do we, as a field, talk about which version of which enzyme you have?
Because every CYP gene comes in dozens of variants in the human population, and we need a way to refer to them without losing our minds.
The convention is called star allele nomenclature, and it's elegant once you get used to it.
Every variant of a gene gets a star, then a number.
The wild-type, fully-functional reference allele is always star one.
So CYP2D6\1 is the reference, fully-functional CYP2D6 enzyme.
CYP2D6\2 is the second-described variant.
CYP2D6\*3 is the third.
And so on, up into the dozens for the most-studied genes — CYP2D6 has well over one hundred star alleles defined.

Each star allele represents a particular combination of variants — usually one or a few SNPs, sometimes a deletion, sometimes a structural change — that occurs in the population at a meaningful frequency, and that has a characterizable functional consequence.
Some star alleles produce a normally-functional enzyme.
Some produce a slow enzyme.
Some produce no enzyme at all.
Some produce extra copies of the gene, which means extra enzyme.
Let me walk you through the CYP2D6 example, because it's the cleanest illustration of how the whole system works.
CYP2D6\*1.** The wild-type.
Codes for a fully-functional CYP2D6 enzyme.
If you have two \*1 alleles, one from each parent, you've got two working copies and you metabolize CYP2D6 substrates at the expected rate.
This is what the textbook patient looks like.
CYP2D6\*2.** Also functional, encodes an enzyme that works essentially normally.
The difference from \1 is a SNP that doesn't meaningfully affect activity.
So functionally, \2 behaves like \*1.
CYP2D6\*4.** This is the classic poor-metabolizer allele in European populations.
It's a single SNP at a splice site that completely abolishes enzyme production.
If you carry a \4 allele, that copy of the gene produces _no functional enzyme_.
About 20% of Europeans carry at least one \4.
And if you're unlucky enough to be \4/\4 — both copies — you have no CYP2D6 function at all.
You're a complete poor metabolizer.
CYP2D6\*5.** A deletion.
The entire gene is gone.
Same functional consequence as \*4 — no enzyme from that copy.
CYP2D6\*10.** Common in East Asian populations, produces an enzyme with reduced activity.
Not zero, just slower.
If you're \10/\10, you're what's called an intermediate metabolizer.
CYP2D6\*17.** Common in African populations, also reduced function.
CYP2D6\*2xN.** This is the ultrarapid metabolizer notation.
The "xN" means the gene has been duplicated N times — sometimes two copies on a single chromosome, sometimes three, sometimes thirteen.
Yes, thirteen.
There are documented cases of patients with thirteen functional copies of CYP2D6 on a single chromosome.
If you carry one of these duplications, you produce dramatically more CYP2D6 enzyme than normal, and you tear through CYP2D6 substrates at remarkable speed.
So you can see how this is going.
Two alleles per person, one from each parent.
The combination — your diplotype — determines your enzyme function.
And the field has developed a standardized way to convert diplotypes into a small set of metabolizer phenotypes.
Let me give you those phenotypes.
There are five of them, and they're the language clinicians use to describe a patient's metabolic status for a given enzyme.
Poor metabolizer (PM).
No functional enzyme.
Both alleles are no-function alleles.
For CYP2D6, this might be a \4/\4 patient.
For substrates of that enzyme, a poor metabolizer cannot metabolize the drug normally.
If the drug is active in its parent form and gets inactivated by the enzyme, a PM accumulates the drug to high blood levels — they're at risk of toxicity at a standard dose.
If the drug is a prodrug that needs the enzyme to be activated, a PM gets no drug effect — the drug doesn't work.
Intermediate metabolizer (IM).
Reduced enzyme function.
One no-function allele plus one normal, or two reduced-function alleles.
The patient metabolizes substrates more slowly than normal, but not zero.
They're somewhere in between.
Normal metabolizer (NM).
Sometimes called "extensive metabolizer" in older literature.
Two functional alleles.
The patient metabolizes the drug at the rate the drug label assumes.
This is what the standard dose is calibrated for.
Rapid metabolizer (RM).
Faster than normal, but not extreme.
Often this is a patient with one fully-functional allele and one allele with elevated activity, or two normal alleles in someone with environmental induction.
Ultrarapid metabolizer (UM).
Dramatically elevated enzyme activity.
Often due to gene duplication, like CYP2D6\*2xN.

For active drugs that get inactivated by the enzyme, an ultrarapid metabolizer chews through the drug so fast that it doesn't reach therapeutic concentrations — the drug effectively doesn't work, even though they're taking it.
For prodrugs, ultrarapid metabolizers convert the prodrug to the active drug so efficiently that they can hit toxic levels of the active form.
Now here's where the analogy from earlier becomes load-bearing.
The poor metabolizer is the slow chef.
Orders pile up at their station.
The drug, which the body is trying to get rid of, just sits in the blood, building up to higher and higher concentrations with each dose, eventually reaching toxic levels.
Or, if the chef's job was to activate a prodrug, the drug never gets activated and nothing happens.
The ultrarapid metabolizer is the fast chef.
They tear through orders so quickly that by the time anyone else can react, the food is gone.
For an active drug, this means the drug never accumulates enough to work — they need higher doses than normal.
For a prodrug, it means they convert the inactive form to the active form so fast that toxic levels of the active drug pile up downstream.

The fast chef is dangerous in a way that's exactly opposite to the slow chef — and which is more dangerous depends entirely on whether the drug they're handling is the active form or the inactive form.
This is the conceptual core of pharmacogenomics.

The genotype tells you the diplotype, the diplotype tells you the phenotype — poor, intermediate, normal, rapid, ultrarapid — and the phenotype tells you what's likely to happen when you give the patient a standard dose of a drug metabolized by that enzyme.
From a single buccal swab worth of DNA, you can predict, before you ever prescribe, which of those five categories the patient falls into for each major enzyme.
And once you know the category, the dose adjustment falls out of published guidelines.
The promise, in a sentence: one genetic test, done once, lets you dose-adjust hundreds of drugs over the patient's lifetime.
